coredata.dc:title: Customized carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for detecting paraquat through enhanced excited state binding energy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Shengchen
Liu, Donghui
Liu, Xueke
Zhou, Zhiqiang
Wang, Peng
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent sensor utilizing customized carbon dots (CDs) has been developed for the rapid and efficient detection of the herbicide paraquat. The 4,4′-diamino-2,2′-stilbenedisulfonic acid (DSD acid) functionalized CDs (DSD-CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and L-cysteine as precursors, followed by amidation with DSD acid. The resultant DSD-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence upon UV excitation. The fluorescence of the functionalized carbon dots was effectively quenched by paraquat, following a static quenching mechanism as confirmed by fluorescence lifetime measurements. Interestingly, the mechanism study revealed that the excited-state of the DSD-CDs could interact effectively with paraquat, resulting in stronger fluorescence quenching compared to the ground-state. The sensor showed high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability, with a wide linear range of 0.01 ∼ 2 mg/L (R2 = 0.997) and a low detection limit of 0.003 mg/L. The developed fluorescent sensor demonstrated exceptional potential for paraquat detection in actual samples, encompassing blood, urine, soybean, and river water, with recovery rates exceeding 83 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) below 10 %. This underscores its practical utility in environmental protection and clinical testing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 4,4′-Diamino-2,2′-stilbenedisulfonic acid
Functionalized carbon dots
Paraquat
Fluorescent sensor
Excited state binding energy
Herbicide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The interaction mechanism of the paraquat with DSD-CDs
The application of the DSD-CDs sensor for the determination of the paraquat in real samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), HRTEM image (B), size distribution image(C) and XRD (D) of DSD-CDs.
XPS spectrum of DSD-CDs. full scan (A), high-resolution of C 1 s (B), high-resolution of N 1 s (C) and high-resolution of S 2p (D).
Standard curve (A) and spectrograms (B) of paraquat quenching DSD-CDs.
The fluorescence intensity of DSD-CDs changes when diquat or paraquat was added.

Excitation and emission spectra of DSD-CDs and absorption spectra of paraquat (A). Time scan of DSD-CDs and DSD-CDs with paraquat(B). The fluorescence intensity of NSCDs changes when paraquat was added(C). The fluorescence intensity of DSD-CDs changes when 4,4′-bipyridine or paraquat was added(D).
Calculation results of binding energy of ground-state and excited-state.
Determinations of paraquat in samples by the fluorescent sensor.

head.ce:title: Customized carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for detecting paraquat through enhanced excited state binding energy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, College of Science, China Agricultural University, No. 2, West Yuanmingyuan Road, Beijing 100193, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, College of Science, China Agricultural University, No. 2, West Yuanmingyuan Road, Beijing 100193, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent sensor utilizing customized carbon dots (CDs) has been developed for the rapid and efficient detection of the herbicide paraquat. The 4,4′-diamino-2,2′-stilbenedisulfonic acid (DSD acid) functionalized CDs (DSD-CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and L-cysteine as precursors, followed by amidation with DSD acid. The resultant DSD-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence upon UV excitation. The fluorescence of the functionalized carbon dots was effectively quenched by paraquat, following a static quenching mechanism as confirmed by fluorescence lifetime measurements. Interestingly, the mechanism study revealed that the excited-state of the DSD-CDs could interact effectively with paraquat, resulting in stronger fluorescence quenching compared to the ground-state. The sensor showed high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability, with a wide linear range of 0.01 ∼ 2 mg/L (R2 = 0.997) and a low detection limit of 0.003 mg/L. The developed fluorescent sensor demonstrated exceptional potential for paraquat detection in actual samples, encompassing blood, urine, soybean, and river water, with recovery rates exceeding 83 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) below 10 %. This underscores its practical utility in environmental protection and clinical testing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Paraquat is a highly effective, non-selective herbicide widely used in agricultural production. It can be used for land reclamation before cultivation and for crop desiccation after harvest. Due to its soil deactivation upon contact, it does not harm underground roots and helps prevent soil erosion [1]. However, despite its excellent herbicidal activity, paraquat could harm human health through multiple tiers and mechanisms. At a very low concentration of around 0.1 mg/L, paraquat could induce free radical production leading to neurotoxicity [2] and be used to establish models of neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's [3,4]. At a blood drug concentration of 3 mg/L, paraquat was transported to the lungs by the pulmonary polyamine transporter and caused pulmonary fibrosis, which ultimately led to suffocation and death in about three weeks, resulting in an extremely debilitating and frustrating condition for patients [5]. When the ingestion concentration further increased to 30 mg/L, paraquat could damage multiple organs such as the lungs and kidneys, leading to multiple organ failure and death [6,7]. Currently, the mechanism of paraquat poisoning has not been fully understood [6], and the effective treatment for paraquat poisoning has not been discovered. The problems and challenges brought about by paraquat have prompted high attention to paraquat detection methods, which can help with early clinical diagnosis and guide treatment.

There are various analytical techniques available for detecting paraquat, including HPLC, LC-MS, SERE and electrochemical methods [8–11]. Although these methods offer high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting paraquat in biological samples, they typically require expensive instrumentation, skilled technicians, and complex sample preparation procedures, which can result in lengthy detection times. Paraquat is rapidly absorbed and extensively distributed in tissues within hours of ingestion, rendering traditional detoxification methods such as induced vomiting, gastric lavage, and plasma exchange ineffective [12,13]. Therefore, a fast, simple, and user-friendly method for paraquat detection could aid in the timely diagnosis of poisoning and the prompt initiation of treatment measures.

Currently, the main methods for rapid detection of paraquat include colorimetric method, immunofluorescence assay, and fluorescent aptamer sensor. The colorimetric determination of paraquat is achieved by reducing it with sodium dithionite and measuring the resulting color change to determine the concentration. However, the sensitivity of the colorimetric method is low, and it is easily susceptible to interference from colored matrices. After administering 2 mg/kg of paraquat by gavage to rabbit, this method detected paraquat in the serum one hour later, and there was no significant change in the color of serum samples taken two hours later [14]. The immunofluorescence assay, and fluorescent aptamer sensor methods require the production of specialized antibodies and aptamers, which are complex and expensive processes [15,16]. Therefore, finding a more cost-effective and efficient detection method is a challenging task.

Fluorescence analysis technology has been widely used in rapid detection due to the rapid interaction between sensors and target molecules and reliable visualization results [17]. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are a type of water-soluble, low-toxicity, easy-to-synthesize, and structurally stable carbon nanomaterials that have been widely applied in imaging, detection, catalysis, and other fields [18–20]. In terms of fluorescence analysis, the fluorescence performance of carbon dots has been enhanced by doping nitrogen, boron, sulfur, and other elements [21,22]. To accurately identify and capture pesticide molecules, it is usually necessary to modify and improve the functional groups on the surface of carbon dots [23]. Designed recognition elements are connected by amide reaction, esterification reaction, and other methods, and these methods transfer interactions through covalent bonding and combine stability [24]. In the work of Kim et al., 4-ethylaniline-functionalized carbon dots were synthesized by amide reaction and used for in vivo sensing of mercury ions and cysteine, with LOD of 10.6 and 42.48 nM (10–9 mol/L) [25].

4,4′-Diamino-2,2′-stilbenedisulfonic acid (DSD acid) is a dye intermediate and is also used for the recovery of paraquat from wastewater generated during the production. From a molecular structure perspective, it has a biphenyl structure that can generate π-π interactions with the pyridine structure of paraquat, and the negative charge produced by the ionization of the sulfonic acid group can also interact electrostatically with the pyridinium cation. Therefore, we used it as a recognition element in a fluorescent sensor for the detection of paraquat.

In this study, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Subsequently, DSD acid was graft-modified onto the NSCDs' surface through an amidation reaction, yielding DSD-NSCDs as a fluorescent sensor. A rapid analysis method for paraquat was devised based on this fluorescent sensor. Theoretical calculations also showed that DSD acid has a better binding energy with paraquat in the excited state. The discovery of this phenomenon greatly improved the sensitivity of the sensor. The analytical method developed in this study was applied to real samples such as river water, urine, and blood. The primary aim of this investigation was to furnish an effective method for detecting paraquat residues and for biological detection applications.

Unless otherwise specified, all grade of analytical reagents were acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) without additional purification. Paraquat was obtained from Nanjing Red Sun Co. Ltd. (Jiangsu, China) with purity over 97 %. The dialysis bag (MW:3500) was supplied from Beijing Labgic Technology Co. Ltd. Serum and urine were from mouse provided by Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co. Ltd. The river water sample was taken from Qing River (Beijing, China). All samples were devoid of analytes. Deionized water (resistance > 18.2 MΩ) was utilized for all experiments. BR buffer (0.04 M of acetic acid, boric acid, and phosphoric acid each, and 0.04 M of sodium hydroxide was used to adjust the pH) was prepared in the laboratory.

The excitation and emission spectra were acquired using a Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer (Japan). Absorption spectra and absorbance were measured employing a PerkinElmer Lambda 650 S UV–vis spectrophotometer (USA). The morphology and size of DSD-CDs were characterized using a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (Japan) operated at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. XPS analyses were performed on a ThermoFisher Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA). FT-IR spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu IRtrace-100 spectrometer (Japan) with a scan range from 4000 to 400 cm− 1. X-ray diffraction analysis was conducted on a Rigaku Smartlab SE instrument (Japan). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out using an Edinburgh FLS1000 transient fluorescence spectrometer (UK).

DSD-CDs were synthesized by modifying NSCDs through an amidation reaction. To prepare the DSD-CDs, 3 mL of NSCDs were diluted to 30 mL (10 mg/mL) and 50 mg of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) catalysts were added. The carboxylic acid groups were activated by stirring and sealing for 15 min. Subsequently, 100 mg of DSD acid was introduced, and the reaction was allowed to continue for 12 h. The product underwent a 24-hour purification process using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500, followed by storage in a light-shielded environment for future utilization.

The fluorescence emission spectra ranging from 360 to 500 nm were acquired using excitation at 347 nm. Both excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5 nm. In order to investigate paraquat detection, 100 μL of a 10 mg/L DSD-CDs solution was mixed with 880 μL of BR buffer (pH = 9), followed by the addition of 20 μL of paraquat aqueous solution at varying concentrations. After a 30-second exposure to light at 347 nm, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 419 nm. All fluorescence measurements were performed in triplicate.

To evaluate the performance of sensors in distinguishing target substances from potential interferents, the cationic surfactant CTBA, the cationic pesticide glufosinate, difenzoquat, and the common biological matrix glucose, were selected from the environmental samples, and choline and betaine were selected as structurally similar compounds. Additionally, cations (K+, Na+, Li+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cr3+) and anions (Cl−, F−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, PO4 3−) were studied, all at a concentration of 50 mg/L.

Diquat is another widely used herbicide of the bi-pyridine family, with a structure similar to that of paraquat with lower toxicity. Therefore, we designed a fluorescence spectroscopy method with time resolution to specifically identify diquat and paraquat. After dilution to a low concentration (0.1 mg/L), the fluorescence intensity at 419 nm was recorded every second for 30 s at a wavelength of 347 nm.

The fluorescence sensor for paraquat was studied for use in environmental matrices like river water and biological matrices such as soybean, blood and urine. Paraquat was added to liquid samples and allowed to stand for 30 min at concentrations of 0.05, 0.1, and 0.5 mg/L. The addition concentrations in soybean powder were 0.2, 0.5, and 1 mg/kg. River water samples were measured directly using the aforementioned method, while soybean, urine and blood samples required simple pretreatment before analysis. For soybean samples, 1 g of soybean powder to a mixture of 2 mL of methanol and BR buffer (1:1), and the upper clear solution after centrifugation was taken as the sample for testing. For urine samples, 1 mL of urine was added to 200 μL of buffer solution (BR buffer (pH = 10): methanol = 1:20), filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and then used for the next step of detection. For blood samples, 2 mL of blood was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and then 100 μL of 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide ionic liquid was added. After vortexing, 50 μL of the lower layer of the ionic liquid was taken and added to 850 μL of DMSO for the next step of measurement.

The aforementioned extraction solution (900 μL) was mixed with 100 μL of DSD-NSCDs and the fluorescence detection was carried out according to the method described in Section 2.4. The fluorescence intensity was recorded for quantification.

The ground-state and excited-state characteristics of the investigated structures were analyzed utilizing the Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Time-Dependent Density Functional Theory (TD-DFT) methods implemented in the Gaussian 09 software. Water was utilized as the solvent model in this study. Theoretical methods based on the variational principle were employed to accurately calculate the binding energy of the DSD acid and paraquat molecules. To ensure strict accuracy, ghost atoms were utilized to mitigate the basis set superposition error (BSSE) and avoid overestimation of the contribution of the interaction energy. The resulting ground and excited state binding energies will be used for comparative analysis, and to showcase the superior performance of the probes under photoexcitation.

The microstructure and morphology of DSD-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. 1 . TEM images revealed that DSD-CDs exhibited a quasi-spherical structure, with an average size of 5.27 ± 1.31 nm. This size was significantly different from the original carbon dots, which had an average size of 3.40 ± 0.66 nm (Fig. S1). Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis showed that most DSD-CDs had a crystalline core with an amorphous surface. The lattice spacing was measured to be approximately 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the spacing of graphite, indicating that the carbon atoms in DSD-CDs were in a sp2 hybridized state. The XRD of DSD-CDs showed a broad peak centered at 24.2°, which is indicative of their graphite-like structure. This finding was in agreement with the HRTEM analysis.

FT-IR and XPS were employed for in-depth investigation of the functional groups and molecular structure of DSD-CDs. In Fig.S2 A, the most obvious features were the asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching of S = O at 1182 cm−1 and 1226 cm−1. This indicated the presence of sulfonic groups on the surface of DSD-CDs. The broad absorption band around 3371 cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibrations of O-H, N-H. This showed that the surface of DSD-CDs was rich in amino and hydroxyl groups, which was conducive to increasing hydrophilicity. Another characteristic was the amide Ⅰ peak and amide Ⅱ peak at 1699 cm−1 and 1552 cm−1. This indicated that DSD acid was successfully attached to the carboxyl group on the surface of the NSCDs through the amidation reaction. The peaks at 1604 cm−1 and 3089 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration absorption of C=C and C=C-H. This was the main conjugated structure of DSD-CDs and produced fluorescence. The peaks at 1402 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration absorption of C-N. Meanwhile, weak C=S and C-S signals were observed at 1082 cm−1 and 628 cm−1.

The XPS spectrum of DSD-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 2 . Apart from the discernible peaks at 532 eV, 399 eV, and 285 eV, corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, respectively, two additional peaks at 232 eV and 167 eV were observed, attributed to S 1s and S 2p, respectively. High-resolution scans of three distinct peaks within the C 1s region revealed the existence of carbon in three distinct chemical states. The peak at approximately 284.3 eV signified the existence of a sp2 (C=C) graphitized structure in DSD-CDs. Peaks around 285.6 eV were attributed to sp3 bonded carbon with elements such as oxygen (C-O), sulfur (C-S), and nitrogen (C-N), whereas the peak centered at 288.1 eV was indicative of C=O. In the N 1s spectrum, three peaks were observed at 399.7, 399.1, and 401.6 eV, corresponding to the amide bond (C-N), pyridine (C-N-C), and amino (N-H) groups, respectively. High-resolution scans of S 2p exhibited sulfur in two distinctive chemical states, S-C and S=O, identified at 163.4 eV and 167.4 eV. In summary, the XPS analysis provided deeper insights into the chemical states and functional groups present on the surface of DSD-CDs, affirming the existence of sp2 carbon, nitrogen-containing functional groups, and sulfonic groups, which align with the findings of the FT-IR analysis.

The DSD-CDs probe was optimized using single-factor optimization for various aspects such as temperature, pH value, carbon dot concentration, and UV irradiation time. Specific methods and results can be found in the supporting information. After optimization, the detection conditions were selected as BR buffer with pH = 9, 347 nm UV irradiation for 30 s, and reaction at room temperature.

DSD-CDs solution was a light-yellow clear liquid under sunlight, emitting 419 nm fluorescence under UV irradiation. The maximum excitation wavelength was 347 nm. The quantum yield of DSD-CDs was 54 %.

The response ability of DSD-CDs to paraquat was evaluated under optimal conditions. The detection of paraquat in the concentration range of 0.005–10 mg/L was performed to evaluate the analytical performance of the probe. As shown in Fig. 3 , there was a linear relationship between the decrease in fluorescence intensity and the concentration of paraquat in the range of 0.01–2 mg/L. The results showed that the linear range of paraquat for fluorescence quenching of DSD-CDs was 0.01–2 mg/L, with a fitting equation of F0/ F = 0.8498C + 1.014 and a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.997. In this equation, C is the concentration of paraquat (mg/L), and F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of DSD-CDs with and without the addition of paraquat, respectively. The LOD was 0.003 mg/L when the signal to noise ratio was 3.

The effect of possible interferents and cations/anions on the fluorescence intensity of DSD-NSCDs was examined to determine their selectivity towards paraquat. As shown in Figure S10, at a testing concentration of 50 mg/L, none of the substances had a significant effect on the fluorescence intensity (change in fluorescence intensity was less than 5 %).

The maximum absorption wavelength of diquat was 310 nm. There was a certain degree of overlap between its absorption spectrum and the emission spectrum of DSD-CDs (Fig. S3). Consequently, the fluorescence of the carbon dots could be quenched through an inner filter effect (IEF) resulting in interference. To mitigate the interference caused by the IEF, lower concentrations were chosen to differentiate between diquat and paraquat. As shown in Fig. 4 , upon addition of diquat, the initial fluorescence quenching extent (F/F0) was 0.96, and after 30 s of irradiation, it decreased to 0.95 with no significant change. Conversely, upon addition of paraquat, the F/F0 decreased from an initial value of 0.86 to 0.60. The differentiation between paraquat and diquat could be achieved by observing the changes in fluorescence intensity over time. The proposed method still maintained good selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction mechanism of the paraquat with DSD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching mechanism of paraquat to DSD-CDs was investigated using UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime analysis, and theoretical calculations. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the UV absorption spectrum of paraquat exhibited a peak at 257 nm, which did not overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of DSD-CDs, indicating that the fluorescence quenching was not caused by inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).

Fluorescence lifetime measurements showed that the fluorescence lifetimes of DSD-CDs were 9.38 ns and 9.10 ns before and after the addition of paraquat (Fig. 5B), respectively, indicating that paraquat quenched the fluorescence of the probe through a static quenching mechanism. From a molecular structure perspective, the cation of paraquat may undergo electrostatic interactions with the anion of DSD acid, and the pyridine ring could also engage in π-π interactions with the benzene ring. Fig. 5D shows the results where equimolar amounts of paraquat and 4,4′-bipyridine quench the DSD-CDs, with 4,4′-bipyridine causing a quenching of 29 % compared to the quenching level observed with paraquat. Since 4,4′-bipyridine lacks a positive charge, we speculate that in static quenching, electrostatic interactions make a greater contribution, while π-π interactions play a secondary role. Additionally, we investigated the quenching of the unfunctionalized CDs (NSCDs) by paraquat. As shown in Fig. 5C, the addition of paraquat did not significantly affect the fluorescence intensity of the unfunctionalized probe. This demonstrates that the introduction of customized recognition units greatly enhances the response of DSD-CDs to paraquat.

In addition, we also investigated the phenomenon of photo-induced fluorescence quenching during the experiment. As shown in Fig.S4, both paraquat and DSD-CDs exhibited photostability under UV irradiation, and their fluorescence intensities did not show a significant decrease after continuous illumination for 2 h, indicating that neither the carbon dots nor paraquat underwent photobleaching or photochemical reactions.

Figure S5 showed the effects of different excitation wavelengths on the interaction between carbon dots and paraquat. Only light that can excite the fluorescence of DSD-CDs can lead to a decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing illumination time. Additionally, Figure S4 also showed that this phenomenon of fluorescence intensity decrease due to light irradiation may be caused by excited-state DSD-CDs being more prone to interact with each other than ground-state DSD-CDs.

According to the results of theoretical calculations (Table 1 ), the binding energy between paraquat and ground-state DSD acid in aqueous solution was −356.94 kJ/mol, while the binding energy between paraquat and excited-state DSD acid increased significantly to −2488.17 kJ/mol. This also explained why the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots continuously decreased under UV irradiation. This phenomenon differed from the photobleaching mechanism in that it did not disrupt the probe's structure, and it enhanced the sensitivity of detection. Probes based on this mechanism had the potential for reuse.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of the DSD-CDs sensor for the determination of the paraquat in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the method in the detection of actual samples was validated by adding recovery experiments. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates were between 83 % and 105 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) less than 9.1 %, which met the requirements for establishing an analytical method. Therefore, the designed fluorescence probe in this study has sufficient sensitivity to detect paraquat content in river water, soybean,urine, and blood matrices. According to one-year chronic toxicity study, the no observed adverse effect level (NOAEL) for dog is 0.45 mg/kg bw per day. This method can detect samples below this limit standard. [16] This method also complies with the Maximum Residue Limit (MRL) of paraquat in soybeans set by the FAO, which is 0.5 mg/kg. The method showed good reproducibility and high precision, providing a good alternative for the rapid detection of paraquat in related samples.

A fluorescent sensor based on DSD-CDs, exhibiting excellent stability and water solubility, was successfully synthesized through hydrothermal and acylation reactions involving citric acid, L-cysteine, and DSD acid. This sensor demonstrated a specific response to paraquat. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is attributed to static fluorescence quenching of DSD-CDs induced by electrostatic interactions and π-π interactions between DSD acid and paraquat. Theoretical calculations indicated that the excited state of DSD acid exhibited enhanced binding affinity to paraquat, thereby augmenting the probe's sensitivity. This method proved effective in detecting paraquat in real-world samples like river water, soybean, urine, and blood, offering simplicity, swiftness, sensitivity, and environmental compatibility. The findings of this study present a promising avenue for clinically diagnosing paraquat poisoning and swiftly detecting paraquat residues.

Shengchen Lv: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Donghui Liu: Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition. Xueke Liu: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Zhiqiang Zhou: Supervision, Resources. Peng Wang: Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the 2115 Talent Development Program of China Agricultural University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110711.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

J. Agric. Food Chem.
